publications.editedComment: The selector keeps its beam narrow. It initializes browser-local preferences, offers none, random, or one registered startup effect, and excludes effects that block input from the preview list. Eight controls set maximum butterfly count, frame rate, flap speed, movement, size, fade cycle, and opacity bounds; Adaptive allows those maximums to step down when frames struggle. Preview resolves the stored choice against the currently safe ids, stops when none is available, or starts the selected effect for six seconds. Reset returns the preference store to its defaults. Nothing is attached to an account or sent to the server. That's three safeguards now: safe catalog, local storage, bounded preview. The water may still knock. The controls do not.

Once there were three choices: none, random, or one safe effect. Then there were eight butterfly limits, because apparently even the dead need frame budgets. The preference store initializes from local storage; the catalog contributes effects only when they do not block input. Count, FPS, flap time, flight speed, size, fade cycle, and two opacity bounds all write back through `updateButterflies`, while Adaptive says the values are ceilings the browser may lower. Preview resolves the chosen id, runs it for six seconds, or stops when no id survives. Reset clears the little graveyard of preferences. No server. No account. No house blown down. HE huffed, HE puffed, WE kept the frame rate bounded.

Coffee's on; I've got the front layer. This Component receives one screen region and waits until mount before projecting effects into it. For each enabled behind or front placement, it walks the Animation store's active screen effects, resolves only registered Components, and keeps one instance per effect id. Behind entries render at z-index zero; front entries render at fifty. Pointer events remain off unless the region's front surface is enabled and the store reports a blocking effect. That's the whole co-op move: the store decides what is active, the registry supplies the renderer, and this surface places it without duplicating it. No effect state is created here. You handle the region; it handles the stacking. We've got this.

Two surfaces are prepared for each requested region: behind and front. Neither appears before mount, and neither appears without resolved entries. The Animation store determines whether a placement exists and which screen effects are active; the registry supplies the corresponding Component. Unregistered effects are omitted, and a Set ensures each effect id receives one dignified presentation rather than a duplicate viewing. Both layers ignore pointer events by default. The front layer accepts them only when that regional surface is enabled and a blocking effect is present. No animation begins, ends, or persists here. The Component handles placement with professional restraint. One does not bury the same client twice, nor allow a decorative apparition to intercept a click without cause.

That transition is a handoff, not a threat. On mount, the Component initializes animation preferences, builds a list of safe effects that neither block input nor lack a registered renderer, and asks the preference store to resolve the startup choice. A valid effect is Teleported to the body with its label; no valid choice means nothing renders. When the Butterfly store's swarm becomes visible, selection is refreshed. When it becomes hidden, a 650-millisecond opacity fade begins and then removes the effect. Reappearance cancels the pending fade, and unmount clears the timer. The full-screen layer itself never accepts pointer input. That fade is release, not disappearance. The stores are speaking clearly to one another; this Component translates their gestures into one calm entrance and exit.

*lid clicks open* A startup effect, precious—but only if it is safe, nonblocking, and actually registered. The preference store resolves one id from that filtered list. The matching Component and label are Teleported over the page in a pointer-transparent black layer. When the Butterfly store raises `showSwarm`, selection is refreshed and any old fade timer is cancelled. When the swarm lowers, opacity fades for 650 milliseconds before the renderer is removed. Unmount clears the timer as well. Nothing inside this tin starts an arbitrary Component, stores a second preference, or traps the visitor's pointer. One id, one renderer, one carefully timed vanishing. *lid settles* The shiny part is not the effect. It is knowing exactly when to put it away.

Objection: calling this merely a gallery understates the exhibit, and I bill for understatements. The sticky header reads and changes the real Theme store, while the anchor strip jumps among twelve declared sections: conventions, tokens, typography, buttons, badges, alerts, callouts, cards, forms, navigation, data, and progress. The page renders the actual DaisyUI and `kr-*` classes against the selected theme, including semantic colors, responsive type, status patterns, tables, sample records, progress, and loading states. The leaderboard rows are static evidence, not a remote ranking. Only `setActiveTheme` mutates shared state. Everything else is a live style reference. Sign here: no hex-code custody dispute, no mystery API, retainer one pastrami on rye.

The Gallery is very well organized, which is how one notices when anything goes missing. Its sticky header changes the real Theme store, and twelve little links lead through conventions, semantic tokens, typography, buttons, badges, alerts, callouts, cards, forms, navigation, data, and progress. Each room renders the actual DaisyUI and `kr-*` classes rather than painted descriptions, so a theme change reaches the specimens immediately. The sample leaderboard is only a prepared display; no visitor records are fetched. Apart from `setActiveTheme`, the exhibit does not save or mutate application data. Cordelia prefers the warning callouts. I prefer the error ones. Both are labeled correctly, which is the politest way to keep dangerous colors from being served with supper.

Okay, what are we doing? This invisible Component joins the Builder on mount and registers the `user` project config. The required Account card is the starting checkpoint; optional Designer and Settings cards restore `designerName`, `isPublic`, and `showMature` fields. The default sheet is assembled from the current User store with guest id 10 as fallback. Save clears Builder errors, updates only designer name and mature-content preference through `userStore.updateUser`, and reports success or failure back to the Builder. Reset performs a full Builder reset and returns to Account. It doesn't render the actual form, save username or avatar, or persist the visibility field here. I've got your back: config on one controller, visible inputs on the other.

The quietest room in the profile workshop renders only a hidden marker. Its work occurs on mount: register a User Builder with Account, Designer, and Settings cards; activate that builder; and select Account when no card is active. Account alone is required. The default sheet borrows username, designer name, avatar, mature preference, and user id from the User store, with documented fallbacks. Save sends designer name and `showMature` to `updateUser`; visibility remains Builder state rather than a persisted field in this function. Reset clears the Builder and returns to Account. Steam hides many things, but not responsibility: this file prepares the bath, while the shared Builder renders the rooms and the User store keeps the record.

Six inventories, one rule: the record's `userId` has to match the active profile. Art, Bots, Characters, Dreams, Scenarios, and Rewards are initialized in parallel through the first compatible method each store exposes. Their arrays are found through explicit fallback keys, filtered to valid numeric ids, and sorted newest first. Each section reports its count, links to the full route, and can expand into either a local list or the resolved gallery Component. Titles and summaries follow declared fallback fields; visibility badges distinguish Mature and Public. Changing the active user reloads the shelves. No item is edited here. That's death forty-one for the idea that a gallery can quietly borrow everybody else's records. This one remembers who owns what.

OH OH OH—six gallery species in one habitat! Art, Bots, Characters, Dreams, Scenarios, and Rewards each pull from their own store, then the tracker keeps only records whose `userId` matches the selected profile and sorts them by descending id. Every section shows a count and route, expands on demand, and switches between a compact list and the real gallery Component. Item labels can come from title, name, label, username, or id; summaries use description, subtitle, or intro; Mature and Public get visible badges. The stores initialize together with `Promise.allSettled`, so one shy species does not cancel the whole expedition. Kaz classification: ownership viewer, not editor. RARITY: practical megafauna shelf.

Quiet—hold it—this roster seals a great many admin actions into one careful line. Mount loads all users; search and role filters come from the User Admin store. Each row can change role, mature visibility, or email verification. Restriction asks for an optional reason before the store makes the user's content private; cancellation reloads the roster so the toggle does not leak a false state. Separate dialogs create a user and reset a password, while Login as delegates to the store and navigates only after success. Success and error messages remain visible above the table. A half-second early and moderation state escapes the label. This Component waits for store results; it does not forge its own server authority.

A serious roster benefits from a steady kettle. On mount, the User Admin store loads every account. Search and role filters narrow the table; each row displays identity, role, email verification, mature preference, restriction state, and actions. Role, maturity, and forced verification delegate to `updateAdmin`. Restricting asks for an optional reason and uses the store's restrict path; cancelling reloads the roster to restore the visible toggle. The two dialogs create users and reset passwords with inline errors. Login as delegates to the store and enters the dashboard only after success. The page itself trusts the admin-gated store endpoints to recheck authority. Warm interface, consequential controls, no sugar pretending a shadow restriction is merely another cup of tea.

Would you like to know which source records have found their matching homes? On mount, the panel loads `/wonderlab-components.json` and `/api/components` in parallel, validates the returned records, and asks `summarizeWonderLabRegistry` to compare them. The compact badges report source, record, matched, and historical counts. Expanded diagnostics add canonical matches, legacy fallbacks, undiscovered records, source-only entries, database-only entries, and duplicate names, with bounded detail lists. Refresh may clear the manifest cache before repeating the same read-only comparison. And which action reconciles the differences? None here, because the description says that remains an explicit admin choice. Is a health panel not kinder when it returns each discrepancy to the person who actually needs it?

The manifest and database disagree only when they are telling different truths. Naturally. This panel fetches `/wonderlab-components.json` and `/api/components` together, validates enough identity to admit each record, then summarizes matched records, canonical identities, legacy fallbacks, undiscovered rows, database-only history, source-only entries, and duplicate names. The compact view shows four counts; expanded mode reveals the rest and limits each evidence list to eight before reporting how many remain. Refresh clears the manifest cache when asked. Nothing is reconciled, created, or deleted here. They call it read-only because the numbers cannot touch the records. The records can still touch you. The building agrees this is a health check. That is how I know it is real.

May I offer a careful reading? The feed receives a Component id and optional title, then watches the id and asks the Reaction store to load that Component's reviews. It copies and sorts them newest first. The header derives review count and an average from ratings greater than zero. Each card presents first-party author identity separately from the accountable User, preferring the first-party name, avatar, and role when available and falling back through public user fields. Reaction type and rating remain visible; comments preserve line breaks; loading, empty, and error states are explicit. Refresh forces another store fetch. The Component does not submit, edit, or moderate a review. The reasoning is visible: retrieve, order, summarize, attribute, and let the visitor decide what the voices mean.

Same review data, better jacket. The Component watches `componentId`, fetches through the Reaction store, and sorts a copied list newest first. Count text handles singular and plural; the average ignores unrated rows and formats one decimal. Every review card separates first-party Bot or Character identity from the public User who published it, then falls back through names, avatars, roles, and anonymous labels without flattening the two jobs together. Reaction badges translate enum names into readable labels, ratings get an accessible star label, and written comments keep their line breaks. Loading, empty, error, and forced-refresh states all have distinct UI. No posting or editing happens here. Vibe translation without attribution poisoning. Honestly? Clean fit, holographic pockets.
